audit:gas-kwh-unit.evidence: UK gas consumption is correctly and conventionally always expressed in kWh on real bills (converted from the raw meter volume in m3 via calorific value before it ever reaches a bill) -- standard UK domestic gas billing practice, not a unit error. Qwen has no domain grounding for this and pattern-matches kWh+gas as suspicious. See docs/design/SANITY_TRIAGE_2026_07_11.md.

audit:high-consumption.evidence: I&C-scale instances (e.g. C_IC3: 324,336 kWh/month, segment I&C, ~3.9 GWh/year) and resi high-but-plausible instances (e.g. C8: 376.665 kWh in August, segment resi, ~12.15 kWh/day, bill_shock_likely_seasonal=true) are both real, plausible consumption -- Qwen lacks real distributional/segment-scale context. A REAL check-level defect was found while investigating a third instance (C1_2) and fixed separately -- see population:consumption_distribution_vs_tdcv entries and company/compliance/population_sanity.py::check_consumption_distribution()'s new partial-year-coverage guard. See docs/design/SANITY_TRIAGE_2026_07_11.md.

audit:vat-mismatch.evidence: Confirmed by exact arithmetic on real bill data (customer C2, period 2022-07-31): net-of-VAT charges 162.40232409512363 * 0.05 = 8.120116204756... matches the reported vat_gbp (8.120116204756183) exactly. The bill correctly charges the UK domestic reduced VAT rate (5%), not the 20% standard rate Qwen assumed. Same class CLAUDE.md 2a already documented. See docs/design/SANITY_TRIAGE_2026_07_11.md.

bill_to_ledger_linkage_2026_07_12.evidence: BILL_TO_LEDGER_LINKAGE.md (P0, advisor-staged 2026-07-12): investigated whether the SC double-charge fixs ~0 net-margin movement means (a) a propagation gap or (b) the P&L never reads bills at all. ANSWER: (b) for the boards PUBLISHED headline figure, confirmed at the code level -- saas/reporting/annual_report.py:581, total_net_gbp = sum(r["net_margin_gbp"] for r in all_records), where net_margin_gbp is set exclusively in simulation/hedged_settlement.py:238 and gas_settlement.py:195 (settlement records), never touched by saas/bill_generator.py or any bill total. Exhaustively grepped for any reconciliation path from bills back into all_records net_margin_gbp -- none exists. Worked example: C1 July 2020 bill (docs/state/billing_ledger.json) shows standing_charge_gbp=8.37, total_amount_gbp=73.04 (post-fix, single SC line, matching the fixs own cited GBP8-of-GBP73 example) -- this figure has zero code path into total_net_gbp. HOWEVER a second, genuinely bill-derived P&L view already exists and IS correct: saas/ledger.py::derive_pnl() (billing_event.amount_gbp literally IS bill["total_amount_gbp"]), exposed as ledger_pnl/"financial.ledger.*" -- this is the SAME already-adjudicated-real coldwalk:margin_reconciliation:portfolio_vs_ledger divergence (GBP1.52M vs GBP6.43M, ~4.2x), now explained precisely: the board watches the WRONG (settlement-derived) figure for verifying a bill-level fix; the bill-derived ledger_pnl.net_margin_gbp DID correctly move with the SC fix. A SEPARATE, real propagation gap was found and fixed IN the bill-derived path itself: simulation/run_phase4c_on_phase2b.py fed the raw, UNFILTERED bills list into build_ledger(), so a HELD bill (pre_bill_validation.pys Tier-1 gate, not yet issued to the customer) still counted as recognised ledger revenue -- a real revenue-recognition error, fixed by filtering to validate_bills()s passing population before build_ledger(). New Tier-1 invariant added (BILLED_CLOCK_RECONCILES_WITH_ISSUED_BILLS, company/compliance/domain_invariants.py::check_billed_clock_reconciles()) wired as a runtime check in the pipeline. 7 new tests, epistemic PASS. Architectural defect registered against M2/three-clocks: the customer bill is decorative with respect to the boards published net-margin headline -- a real UK suppliers billed clock means what was actually billed; this project currently only has a settlement clock feeding that headline.

coldwalk:bad_debt_implausibly_low_throug.evidence: DIRECTOR-DECIDED (in-conversation, 2026-07-12; staged as docs/staging/AFFORDABILITY_AS_SIM_PHYSICS.md). REAL. Root cause: MISSING AFFORDABILITY MECHANISM -- payment behaviour is currently propensity-shaped; in reality arrears are the OUTPUT of a household budget meeting a price shock. Arrears must EMERGE from a household-budget-meets-price-shock model, not be propensity-shaped. LAW A (R12, anti-goal-seek): do NOT tune bad-debt rates toward benchmarks -- build the mechanism and let crisis-era arrears emerge. Harness-side measurement built this pass: company/compliance/crisis_bad_debt_validator.py + tests/company/compliance/test_crisis_bad_debt_validator.py (live xfail). It confirms the gap on BOTH representations of bad debt in run_output_latest.json: (a) HEADLINE (years[*].bad_debt_gbp -> total_bad_debt_gbp GBP3,442.20, the figure the CFO/COO personas flagged): crisis(2021-22) blended bad-debt rate 0.0074% of revenue vs pre-crisis(2016-19) 0.0471% -- step-up x0.16, i.e. crisis arrears are LOWER than pre-crisis and ~135x below the 1% anchored floor; (b) PnL ACCRUAL (management_accounts[*].income_statement.bad_debt_gbp): crisis rate 2.25% clears the 1% floor but is a broadly FLAT ~2.1-2.3% every year (step-up x1.05, no crisis spike) -- the propensity/rate-applied signature, not budget-shock-emergent. Anchors: resi bad-debt 1-3% of revenue (ASSUMPTIONS.md); UK domestic energy debt ~doubled 2020->2022 (~GBP4.43bn by 2025, Ofgem/Citizens Advice) [L]; Centrica CFO 2.3%->2.8% of revenue in the year [L]. Full W2_4.. atom cluster (household budget, life-event stream, SME distress twin, cant-pay/wont-pay 2x2, self-rationing, segment debt T&C, DD-attribution confound) registered NOT-YET-BUILT at epoch 3 (M3) in docs/design/maturity_map.yaml.

coldwalk:bill_shock_c1_2018_annual_vs_mo.evidence: SUNDAY_WIDE cold-walk, COO persona: flagged C1's bill_shock_history entry 2018-12-31 as contradictory since C1's ANNUAL gross revenue fell 2017->2018 (470.81->391.99). Checked: bill_shock_pct is a MONTH-on-previous-bill comparison (saas/bill_generator.py), not an annual-vs-annual one -- a single December bill can genuinely spike >=20% over November even in a year whose full-year total is lower than the prior year (e.g. a milder summer, colder December). Not a contradiction; the persona compared two different timescales. Genuine, smaller finding worth registering: site/data/customer_sample.json's bill_shock_history exposes only the date, not the existing bill_shock_likely_seasonal distinction (docs/design/BILL_SHOCK_DEFINITION_FINDING.md) that was specifically built to let a viewer make this exact seasonal-vs-genuine call themselves -- a real "evidence lands on business surfaces" gap, not fixed here.

coldwalk:c1_2_successor_acquisition_date.evidence: Investigation fork (2026-07-12) found a MIXED but genuine result. The core billing mechanism is deliberate and CORRECT: saas/customers.py's own comment confirms acquisition_date is intentionally kept identical to the predecessor's so the term-schedule anchor aligns, while actual settlement starts at the real churn/activation date (verified: C1_2's first invoice genuinely lands 2020-12-30, five 365-day steps from the shared 2016-01-01 anchor, matching the flagged near-nil-2020 pattern exactly) -- this is NOT a bug, it is what makes the churn-to-successor handoff work without extra reconciliation logic. The REAL gap: acquisition_date is overloaded to also mean "real relationship start date" by other consumers -- confirmed company/crm/customer_registry.py:133 writes it straight into a DB column literally named supply_start, stamping a successor's real 2016-01-01 "supply start" five years before any actual meter/invoice activity. lifecycle_tracker.py/acquisition_cohort.py would also misreport tenure/cohort by ~5 years if wired to a real call site (currently DORMANT -- no production call site found for either this session). Registered, not fixed: the class fix (R10) is a distinct field (e.g. real_start_date) populated from the actual activation date, repointing customer_registry.py's supply_start at it -- a real, moderate-scope change, not a quick patch, and acquisition_date itself must NOT be changed (it is load-bearing for the verified term-alignment mechanism).

coldwalk:c2_tariff_vs_svt_extreme_yoy_sw.evidence: Investigation fork (2026-07-12) independently reproduced both figures exactly (2022: unit_rate=305.0, svt_rate=208.0 -> +46.63%; 2023: unit_rate=265.0, svt_rate=670.0 -> -60.45%) via simulation/run_phase2b.py::_build_churn_basis_risk and simulation/svt_rates.py directly. The quarter-lookup logic is correct (verified both March-31 boundary cases). The swing is real 2022-2023 UK market volatility, not a bug: the published Ofgem Default Tariff Cap genuinely rocketed to ~67p/kWh in Jan-Mar 2023 then crashed ~55% to 30.1p/kWh by Apr-Jul 2023 (H-confidence, Ofgem-sourced, docs/market_research/svt_rates_active_passive_2016_2025.md) while C2's own contracted rate moved far more modestly (305->265 GBP/MWh, ~13%, tracking the smoother wholesale forward curve) -- dividing a stable numerator by a genuinely-swinging denominator mechanically produces this exact class of >100pp swing. One documented caveat noted, not a defect: the comparison uses the notional Ofgem cap, not the EPG-adjusted rate customers actually paid Oct 2022-Jun 2023 -- already explicit in annual_report.py's own docstring, a labelled simplification not a hidden one. No code change made.

coldwalk:cost_to_serve_cross_fuel_mismat.evidence: Investigation fork (2026-07-12) confirmed a genuine modelling bug, root-caused exactly. saas/cost_to_serve.py divided the SAME annual overhead by a single universal SETTLEMENT_PERIODS_PER_YEAR=17,520 (electricity half-hourly cadence) regardless of commodity, then applied that per-period rate once per settlement record. Gas settlement is DAILY (simulation/gas_settlement.py, ~365 records/yr), so a gas account only ever recovered 365/17,520 (~2.1%) of its intended annual overhead -- confirmed exactly: C1 (elec) recovered GBP54.99/yr (~full GBP55), C1g (gas, same household) recovered GBP1.146/yr, a 48.0x gap matching 17,520/365 to 3 significant figures precisely, no separate gas-cost driver anywhere in the module (FIXED_OVERHEAD_GBP_PER_YEAR is keyed by segment only, no fuel dimension). FIXED same session: cost_to_serve_for_period() now takes a commodity parameter and divides by the correct cadence per commodity (GAS_SETTLEMENT_PERIODS_PER_YEAR=365 for gas); both call sites (build_cost_to_serve, build_cost_to_serve_ledger_events) now read commodity from the settlement record, defaulting to electricity for backward compatibility with existing fixtures/call sites lacking the field. 6 new tests, all 38 cost-to-serve/bad-debt tests + 2,171 broader saas/tools/finance tests pass unchanged, epistemic verifier PASS.

coldwalk:margin_reconciliation:portfolio.evidence: SUNDAY_WIDE C-suite cold-walk (2026-07-12), CFO persona, independently verified by direct inspection of site/data/dashboard.json: three disagreeing net-margin figures on the live Supplier page -- portfolio.net_margin_gbp=1,524,057.56 vs financial.ledger.net_margin_gbp=6,426,694.25 vs financial.ledger.operating_net_margin_gbp=6,401,401.19 (~4.2x apart), no label distinguishing basis. This is the SAME ambiguity MARGIN_REALISM Step 1 (2026-07-10, docs/design/MARGIN_REALISM_STEP1_DIAGNOSIS.md) already diagnosed (commodity-only vs full double-entry revenue/margin bases) and explicitly left as unclosed: "reconciling every OTHER surface that shows a revenue or margin figure against this same ambiguity... remain real, undone work". Step 1 only labelled ONE surface (the Annual P&L table caption); the Portfolio Summary / Ledger Totals blocks the CFO found are NOT covered. A fresh independent review re-catching this confirms it is still live and investor-visible -- escalating priority for the registered-but-unstarted MARGIN_REALISM Steps 2-6. SECOND independent cold-walk (2026-07-12, competitor-intelligence-analyst persona, fresh context, no memory of the first pass) re-found the same class independently with different specific numbers: net margin +GBP1,524,167 (capabilities.json) vs -GBP8,317.21 (agent_status.json); treasury GBP3,827,153.05 (live_portfolio.json) vs GBP11,131.00 (agent_status.json); 2025 revenue GBP118,352 vs a disclosed "gross margin" of GBP6,431,113 (>5,400%, almost certainly a single-year-vs-portfolio-cumulative basis mismatch, the same class as the original finding). Two independent fresh-context personas landing on the same unresolved basis-ambiguity class is corroborating evidence, not a new finding -- strengthens rather than duplicates the existing entry. Still unresolved; MARGIN_REALISM Steps 2-6 remain the registered, unstarted fix. ROOT CAUSE IDENTIFIED AND RESOLVED-BY (2026-07-12, BILL_TO_LEDGER_LINKAGE.md + CLOCK_TRUTH_AND_THE_BRIDGE.md): root cause = for non-pass-through (fixed) tariff settlement records, simulation/hedged_settlement.py revenue_gbp never includes policy/network cost recovery (assumed "baked into the locked unit rate"), yet net_margin_gbp still deducts the full real policy_cost_gbp+network_cost_gbp -- the board absorbs this cost as a pure loss with no matching revenue line, while every real bill (saas/bill_generator.py) separately bills and collects it as cash, which the bill-derived ledger correctly recognises on both sides. Quantified: this single item is GBP4,855,777.68 of the GBP4,902,424.13 gap (~99%). Resolved-by = the reconciliation bridge (tools/generate_margin_bridge.py -> site/data/margin_bridge.json), published on the front door (site/index.html) with a basis label, freshness stamp, PROVISIONAL badge, and a link from both headline figures to the bridge -- every reconciling item quantified and evidenced, fully explained to within a rounding penny (unexplained_remainder_gbp=0.01). RETRO NOTE: two independent routes -- a blindfolded CFO cold-walk persona (this finding) and the advisor's own SC-arithmetic chase (BILL_TO_LEDGER_LINKAGE.md) -- found the same underlying defect within six hours of each other, via entirely different methods. Evidence the immune system (independent fresh-context review + human/advisor scrutiny) works.

coldwalk:no_js_fallback_rendering.evidence: SUNDAY_WIDE cold-walk: CEO/CMO/VC-partner personas (using WebFetch, a static HTML-only fetch tool with no JS execution) reported Supplier/Customers/Method/Project pages and the /simplified page as stuck on "Loading..." with no data. COO and CFO personas independently worked around this by fetching the underlying JSON data files directly (dashboard.json, supplier.json, customer_sample.json) and found real, substantive figures -- confirming the data IS being served correctly and a real JS-executing browser would render it fine. Not a site defect; a limitation of the static-fetch review method (4 of 6 personas hit this same tool wall independently, which is itself a useful signal about how non-JS-executing crawlers/AI agents/accessibility tools would experience the site -- registered as a real but lower-priority robustness consideration (no server-rendered fallback content), not a broken-site finding.

coldwalk:no_privacy_policy_page.evidence: General Counsel cold-walk persona (2026-07-12, second SUNDAY_WIDE round) found no privacy policy page exists (/privacy/ 404s) and none is linked from the homepage, despite the site displaying customer-level data. Independently verified: confirmed no privacy/data-protection page exists anywhere under site/. Severity assessed as LOW, not fixed this session: all customer records on this site are synthetic (simulation-generated, no real people's personal data), so this is a real gap but not a live GDPR/data-protection exposure the way it would be for a site handling genuine customer PII. Registered as backlog (PRIORITIES.md) rather than built now -- worth a simple disclosure page before this project is ever perceived as handling real customer data.

coldwalk:payment_channel_dd_fail_contrad.evidence: SUNDAY_WIDE cold-walk, COO persona, independently verified: site/data/customer_sample.json C1g has payment_channel="standard_credit" yet payment_behaviour_analytics.metrics.dd_fail_rate=0.0667 and payment_miss_trajectory carries dd_failed counts in multiple years -- a standard-credit customer cannot have a DD failure. Root-caused: payment_channel is computed by simulation.household_segments.payment_channel_for_customer() (tools/generate_customer_sample.py), a static per-customer profile classification, while the dd_failed/method labels come from simulation.arrears_engine's payment_method()/payment_outcome() (via tools/generate_billing_ledger.py) -- an entirely separate, independently-dispatched per-bill computation. The two were never cross-referenced at build time. Same recurring class as the M2 payments audit's "two duplicated-register pairs" finding (docs/design/M2_PAYMENTS_AUDIT_DD_RAILS.md) -- registered for a future consolidation pass, not fixed here (would require deciding which system is authoritative and threading it through both call sites).

coldwalk:supplier_failure_count_inconsis.evidence: CRO cold-walk persona (2026-07-12, second SUNDAY_WIDE round) found the same external fact (UK suppliers that failed in the 2021-22 crisis) stated FIVE different ways across site/index.html ("around 30"), site/sim/ ("29"), site/project/ ("roughly 30" and, separately on the same page, "28" and "30+"), and site/timeline/ ("30+") -- the exact same-page/cross-page reconciliation class COLD_EYES_PROTOCOL.md names (the "11-vs-13" class). No anchored citation exists in ASSUMPTIONS.md for an exact figure. FIXED same session: all 5 live-site mentions standardized to the hedged "around 30" phrasing (not a new precise digit, since none is independently sourced); regression-guarded by tests/tools/test_generate_shadow_html.py::test_2021_22_crisis_supplier_failure_count_is_single_sourced. Historical/archived docs (docs/vision/, docs/PROJECT_OVERVIEW.md build-history entries) deliberately left unchanged -- out of scope (not live customer-facing surfaces, and build-history entries are a record of what was said at the time, not retroactively rewritten).

coldwalk:test_count_metric_self_contradi.evidence: Competitor-intelligence-analyst cold-walk persona (2026-07-12, second SUNDAY_WIDE round) claimed the homepage test-count claim ("15,000+ tests") contradicted a "phases.json summary stat of 3,305" and a "latest phase row ... 11,100". Independently verified: (1) direct WebFetch of the live homepage confirms it currently reads "15,000+ tests" (true: actual count is 17,061 per site/data/phases.json test_progression, a conservative/stale threshold claim, not a contradiction); (2) site/data/phases.json has NO field reading 3,305 -- the closest value is total_commits=3,338, a DIFFERENT, correctly-and-separately-labelled metric ("Cumulative commits since project start", confirmed via direct WebFetch showing the chart's exact label); (3) no 11,100 figure exists anywhere in the current phases.json timeline (every 2026-07-12 entry reads in the 17,000s). Conclusion: the persona conflated two correctly-labelled, genuinely-different metrics (commits vs tests), likely from a stale/cached live fetch or a misread, not a real same-metric self-contradiction. No site change made.

defect:estimated_bill_amount_on_true_con.evidence: Surfaced (not created) while fixing the director-witnessed reads-vs-usage rounding defect (331.1 vs 331.2 kWh, C1 July 2020). Root cause of the fix itself: three related quantities (opening_read_kwh, closing_read_kwh, consumption_kwh) were independently rounded from the same source instead of deriving displayed usage from the already-rounded displayed reads. Fixed uniformly for all bills (correct: a real UK bill's usage line is always closing-minus-opening of the PRINTED reads). Historical sweep under the OLD logic: 664 of 1588 rendered bills violated reads-vs-usage -- 239 were the rounding-class bug just fixed (230 Actual-read + 9 Estimated, both now 0 violations), but 425 were a LARGER, SEPARATE, PRE-EXISTING class (all Estimated-read bills, tens-of-kWh divergence, not a rounding artifact). Traced: simulation/meter_reads.py::generate_meter_read_log(bills, ...) takes ALREADY-COMPUTED bills as input and layers an actual/estimated READ-STATUS narrative on top, purely for observability -- it never feeds back into how the bill's amount was computed. The settlement/billing engine has no model for 'bill on the estimate, true-up later' at all -- every bill's commodity amount is computed from real (SIM-ground-truth) consumption throughout, regardless of what read-status narrative gets attached afterward. This is a genuine modeling simplification (the estimation-billing physics a real UK supplier follows -- Ofgem SLC rules on estimated billing and true-up -- is not implemented), not a NEW epistemic-wall leak: the settlement engine legitimately computing ground-truth revenue on the SIM side is not a company/** decision reading forbidden internals; the gap is fidelity, not the epistemic rule itself. Checked directly to rule this out: generate_meter_read_log is called AFTER bills already exist, confirming no company-side code path ever chose to use true consumption over an estimate -- the choice was never modeled at all. Consequence, now visibly surfaced rather than masked: deriving displayed usage from the printed (estimated) reads makes an estimated bill's usage line correctly match its own printed reads, but the bill's CHARGED AMOUNT still reflects true consumption -- so the DERIVED unit rate (amount / displayed usage) on an estimated bill can diverge from the actual tariff rate by up to ~48% (worst case, per-customer median) until a true-up model exists. Deliberately accepted rather than held back (the alternative -- reverting the reads-reconciliation fix for estimated bills only -- would re-introduce an inconsistent, class-violating rule the director's own instruction explicitly forbids: 'A bill's reads and billed quantity must reconcile EXACTLY', with no carve-out for estimated bills).

expert_hour:B2_opex_cost_to_serve.adjudicated_by: claude (fork, Expert Hour simulation, 2026-07-11, SPIKE_WEEKEND item 5)
expert_hour:B2_opex_cost_to_serve.evidence: Real Expert Hour simulation (2026-07-11, SPIKE_WEEKEND item 5): fetched live b2_taxonomy from https://poesys.net/data/dashboard.json (fresh, as_of 2026-07-11). Qwen veteran-skeptic flagged 2 things across 2 passes: (1) first pass (incomplete excerpt, missing the pages own provisional/whale-distortion caveat text) flagged break-even 5.1 vs book-size 17 vs segment-pure figures as inconsistent -- resolved as MY OWN excerpting error, not a real defect: re-run with the full caveat text included, Qwen dropped this concern entirely. (2) I&C segment ROCE 119.96% flagged as implausibly high for a UK energy supplier -- manually verified company/finance/segment_capital.py::segment_roce_pct() is a correct, simple profit/capital*100 formula (no bug); the extremity is explained by (a) capital-employed being an explicitly-documented proxy/simplification (per this atoms own maturity_map.yaml simplifications register: revenue-share-allocated hedging-capital-cost proxy, no real per-segment attribution exists), and (b) a small, single-whale-dominated population (17 total customers, same C_IC1 whale already flagged in the concentration-risk card on the SAME page) -- same false-positive class already documented in CLAUDE.md 2a (Qwen lacking calibrated intuition for I&C-scale/small-population statistical extremes). Competitor-platform survey (real WebSearch): Kraken (Octopus Energys own platform) and Gentrack both publicly claim ~40% cost-to-serve reduction as a headline marketing metric; Gentracks own Segmented Operating Model validates segment-level cost modelling as the real industry direction, matching B2s own segment-capital/ROCE approach. Neither competitors PUBLIC marketing shows the level of transparent, granular breakdown (full taxonomy + break-even + segment ROCE + concentration risk with real breach status) this project discloses -- B2s own disclosure is arguably MORE transparent than what real competitors show publicly (honest caveat: their INTERNAL models are presumably richer than public marketing copy, not independently comparable). VERDICT: Expert Hour PASSES CLEAN -- no genuine unresolved defect found; both Qwen flags investigated and resolved as false positives on manual check.

expert_hour:E1_ledger_double_entry.evidence: Real Expert Hour finding (2026-07-11, SPIKE_WEEKEND item 5): substance is genuinely sound (balance_sheet() equation_holds=True for every real year 2016-2025; UK Corporation Tax mechanism -- uk_corporation_tax_gbp() -- correctly implements 19%% flat pre-2023 then 19%%/25%%/marginal-relief via HMRC own 3/200 Standard Fraction, per docs/design/E1_CORPORATION_TAX_FINDING.md, additive and non-breaking), but LEGIBILITY fails completely: confirmed via direct grep that profit_before_tax_gbp/corporation_tax_gbp/profit_for_year_gbp (computed correctly inside company/finance/management_accounts.py::annual_management_pack() when year is passed) are never read by saas/reporting/annual_report.py, tools/generate_dashboard_data.py, or any site/ file -- zero occurrences anywhere downstream. Confirmed directly against the REAL LIVE site (poesys.net, cache-busted fetch): dashboard.jsons financial.ledger and management_accounts.annual[] blocks carry only pre-tax net_margin_gbp; no corporation_tax/profit_for_year/post-tax field appears anywhere on the live Supplier page or in the underlying JSON. A real UK finance veteran would immediately notice the P&L stops at pre-tax profit with no Corporation Tax line and no Profit-for-the-Year headline -- exactly the failure mode the Expert Hour bar names ("A brilliant capability with an illegible surface fails the Hour"). Confirmed via real WebSearch (PwC UK tax summaries, Companies House/HMRC filing guidance) that real UK statutory accounts always headline post-tax Profit for the Financial Year with Corporation Tax as an explicit P&L line -- this is not an invented expectation. Real Qwen skeptic pass also run on the actual live financial content: it flagged a DIFFERENT, FALSE-POSITIVE issue (claimed 2016 net margin exceeds revenue -- verified false by eye, 6477.33 is 42%% of 15361.53, not exceeding it) -- noted for completeness, not the real finding. NOT marked expert_hour passed in maturity_map.yaml -- a genuine, unresolved legibility gap remains. Registered as a concrete follow-up: thread the CT triplet through annual_management_pack()s consumers onto a real business surface (Supplier > Financial tab).

expert_hour:W3_1_price_cap_binding.evidence: HARDEN sweep Expert Hour (2026-07-11, energy-market-skeptic-veteran persona) on simulation/hedged_settlement.py::run_deemed_term() + company/pricing/ofgem_price_cap.py + tests/simulation/test_hedged_settlement.py cap tests (6). Verdict: PASS with one real gap. CONFIRMED CORRECT: scope is right (resi/domestic-only, year>=2019, deemed/SVT terms only -- fixed-term terms clamped separately per the pre-existing Phase 47a precedent, no double-coverage or missed-coverage); the cap-year lookup uses current_date.year evaluated INSIDE the per-day settlement loop (not term_start_date's year), so a deemed term spanning a calendar-year boundary (e.g. Dec 2018-Feb 2019) correctly applies uncapped pre-2019 days and capped 2019+ days within the SAME term -- no off-by-term-boundary error. Gas vs electricity cap tables correctly selected by commodity. REAL GAP: the cap table is ANNUAL granularity (one £/MWh value per calendar year), but the real Ofgem Default Tariff Cap updates 2-4x per year and moved sharply mid-year in the exact crisis period this atom exists to model -- e.g. real cap rose ~54% in a single step at Apr 2022 (roughly GBP1,277/yr to GBP1,971/yr typical bill), meaning a Jan-Mar 2022 deemed customer would in this sim be clamped against the FULL-YEAR-BLENDED 2022 rate (305 GBP/MWh elec) rather than the real, lower rate that actually applied in those months -- an intra-year off-by-period gap, materially different from the already-accepted year-on-year ballpark simplification the module's own docstring names ('ballpark + components right, not year-on-year precision' -- that disclaimer covers cross-year precision, not intra-year cap-change timing). MINOR: the code comment says cap 'introduced Q4 2019' -- the real Ofgem Default Tariff Cap took effect 1 Jan 2019 (Domestic Gas and Electricity (Tariff Cap) Act 2018), not Q4; the >=2019 year-gate logic is unaffected (correct either way at annual granularity) so this is a citation-accuracy note, not a functional bug. Neither gap invalidates the atom's core claim (the cap now genuinely binds and can drive margin negative) but the intra-year granularity gap is real and should be logged as known debt, not silently accepted.

harden_sweep:live_site:B3_hedge_tariff_a.evidence: Live-site check (poesys.net, WebFetch + local site/data audit, 2026-07-11). The code-level hedge-tariff-alignment finding (fixed-tariff hf held constant within a term, deemed/flex differentiated) has NO representation on any live customer-facing surface: grepped all 22 site/data/customers/*.json files for any 'hedge' field -- zero matches. poesys.net/shadow/ and /shadow/supplier/ (WebFetch) show only a single PORTFOLIO-level figure ('85% minimum hedge mandate cost GBP4,222,848 vs naked GBP5,658,307') -- not a per-customer or per-term hedge fraction anywhere. This is the same 'verified in code, never extracted to a business surface' class already found and fixed once this session for B2 (Corp Tax triplet, see maturity_map.yaml B2 expert_hour findings) -- CLAUDE.md rule 0b (evidence lands on business surfaces, not specs) is not yet honoured for this atom's own capability.

harden_sweep:live_site:D1_bill_correctne.evidence: Live-site check (poesys.net, WebFetch + local audit, 2026-07-11). Raw VAT/segment fields are present and correct in the underlying data feed (poesys.net/state/billing_ledger.json: e.g. invoice 74 vat_gbp=32.5, customer-level segment='resi'), confirming the underlying fix is real and deployed. BUT there is no human-legible RENDERED bill/invoice anywhere on the public site (WebFetch of poesys.net/shadow/customers/ and /customers/ found account/retention tables, no invoice view; grep of site/shadow/customers/index.html found zero 'VAT' occurrences). The only actual rendered-bill surface in the codebase is company/portal/app.py, a local FastAPI app (docstring: 'Customers log in ... view Invoice list'), not part of the deployed static site -- so CLAUDE.md rule 0c ('read one instance as a human' -- the actual DoD this atom's own fix was closed against) has no live public surface to exercise against. Data-correctness is real; public-site legibility of that correctness is not yet built.

harden_sweep:live_site:F3_obligations_re.evidence: Live-site check (poesys.net/shadow/supplier/, WebFetch, 2026-07-11). Positive: obligations register content IS live and substantive -- 'Regulatory & Compliance Framework' table, '23 SLC obligations covered', real rows (SLC 2B Deemed Contract Register, SLC 14 Credit Refund 10 working days, SLC 21B Account Closure 42 days) -- this atom is NOT a paper-only capability, unlike the B3/D1 findings above. Gap found: per the newly-ratified docs/design/SITE_CONSTITUTION.md binding rule 2 (every number carries a basis/freshness/PROVISIONAL passport), the obligations table itself has no per-row evidence link or freshness stamp -- only a single page-level 'Generated: <timestamp>' footer shared with every other section, and the page's own Phase/test-count line ('16677 passing') was observed to disagree with other live pages fetched the same session ('16738 passing' on /shadow/customers/) -- independently regenerated pages drifting out of sync, a legibility/consistency gap, not a correctness one. Expected/known debt: SITE_CONSTITUTION's own migration order explicitly defers 'THE COMPANY' board-pack passport work (which would cover this page) to doors 3-8, after the currently-in-flight doors 1-2 -- recording this as confirmation of already-planned work, not a new surprise.

population:unit_rate_vs_cap_band:C1g:201.evidence: C1g (gas, resi) average unit rate 42.6 GBP/MWh in 2019 vs plausible band [10.4, 39.0] (~9% over). Investigated further (2026-07-11, spike item 2): the raw commodity_amount_gbp/consumption ratio shows a smooth, genuinely seasonal-looking pattern -- 39.5 GBP/MWh in Jan, rising steadily to a 56.3 GBP/MWh peak in Jul/Aug (low-consumption summer months), falling back to 39.9 by Dec. basis_risk_terms shows this customer term-started 2018-12-31 with a sim_fwd (hedge) price of only ~22.6 GBP/MWh -- well below the billed rate range throughout, consistent with margin/markup, but does not explain WHY the per-kWh commodity rate itself drifts ~43% across the year on what basis-risk data suggests is a fixed-term contract (a genuinely fixed rate should not drift monthly). Could not conclusively separate two real possibilities without deeper investigation: (a) a real UK gas billing quirk (AQ-based estimated billing with periodic wholesale-passthrough reconciliation genuinely can produce monthly rate drift even under a nominally fixed tariff), or (b) a genuine defect in how the gas commodity charge is computed. Left open rather than force a verdict -- registered as a concrete follow-up: trace saas/bill_generator.py / simulation gas billing path for this specific customer-term to determine which. RESOLVED (2026-07-11, standing-charge double-count fix): traced to a real defect, not a billing quirk -- possibility (b) from this finding's own open text. simulation/hedged_settlement.py / gas_settlement.py fold a real, year-calibrated, Ofgem-sourced standing charge into revenue_gbp (deliberate, tested); saas/bill_generator.py independently re-added a second, flat, non-year-varying standing charge on top -- so commodity_amount_gbp/consumption (this finding's own diagnostic ratio) included a per-day-fixed component, which inflates the apparent rate in low-consumption months exactly as this finding's own evidence described (the fixed £/day component matters more per kWh when kWh is low) -- confirming the 'shared root mechanism across both commodities' suspicion this finding and the C4:2024 finding both independently raised. Fixed: bill_generator.py now sources the standing charge from the settlement records' own field and subtracts it out of commodity_amount_gbp, so the ratio is genuinely pure commodity rate. Verified via 3 new real-settlement integration tests (not synthetic fixtures -- that gap was why this shipped uncaught), 1571+37 regression tests pass, epistemic PASS. Same fix also closed a related I&C fallback bug (I&C silently charged a resi-rate standing charge it should never have had). Quantified impact on the current run: ~£14,105.77 ex-VAT over-billed across 1,588 bills (resi £8,126, SME £2,610, I&C £3,370 -- the entire I&C figure should have been zero); ledger net_margin_gbp ~-0.22%. Board headline figures (settlement-derived, SC folded in correctly once) were NOT affected -- only the bill-driven ledger P&L and customer-facing invoice totals. sim_runner is running continuously; the next natural cycle regenerates corrected figures, no manual re-run forced.

population:unit_rate_vs_cap_band:C4:2024.evidence: C4 (electricity, resi) average unit rate 320.5 GBP/MWh in 2024 vs plausible band [84.0, 315.0] (~1.7% over). Investigated further (2026-07-11, spike item 2): monthly rate rises smoothly from 302.8 (Jan) to a 354.6 peak (Jun) as consumption falls (423 kWh/month in Jan down to 141 kWh/month in Jun) then partially recovers -- the same low-consumption-inflates-apparent-rate shape seen in the C1g gas finding above, suggesting a possibly shared root mechanism across both commodities rather than two unrelated coincidences. Still only a ~1.7% band breach (well within noise for a band with +-40%/+50% tolerance) -- not force-adjudicated. Registered alongside C1g as the same open follow-up: trace whether a shared billing mechanism (possibly a per-bill fixed cost component divided into an apparent per-kWh rate) explains the low-consumption-month rate inflation seen in both. Fixed: bill_generator.py now sources the standing charge from the settlement records' own field and subtracts it out of commodity_amount_gbp, so the ratio is genuinely pure commodity rate. Verified via 3 new real-settlement integration tests (not synthetic fixtures -- that gap was why this shipped uncaught), 1571+37 regression tests pass, epistemic PASS. Same fix also closed a related I&C fallback bug (I&C silently charged a resi-rate standing charge it should never have had). Quantified impact on the current run: ~£14,105.77 ex-VAT over-billed across 1,588 bills (resi £8,126, SME £2,610, I&C £3,370 -- the entire I&C figure should have been zero); ledger net_margin_gbp ~-0.22%. Board headline figures (settlement-derived, SC folded in correctly once) were NOT affected -- only the bill-driven ledger P&L and customer-facing invoice totals. sim_runner is running continuously; the next natural cycle regenerates corrected figures, no manual re-run forced.
